title: Chapter 5
text: Chapter Two R onin crouched, pressing his back to the overturned bus. The dirt that had accumulated around and inside of it would stop the bullets fired by the two reavers, but it was only a matter of time before they flanked him and nullified his cover . “Your salvage, dustwalker!” one of them called, his voice underrun with a digital crackle. “It’s ours whether we end you or not !” At least one synth, then. Even if they were both bots, their first shots had missed, which meant they weren’t operating at peak performance. Ronin wasn’t either, after so many years in the Dust, but his optics were still fully functional . The reavers only had three main avenues of approach, if they chose to attack — around either side of the bus, or over the top. Ronin’s processors ran simulations on all three. There were countless potential variations, despite the limited paths, and none likely to result in minimal personal damage . Wind swept over the wasteland, rustling the tufts of brown grass growing randomly on the dunes and spattering Ronin with grit. Adjusting his audio receptors, he picked out the quiet crunch of dirt beneath the reavers’ boots as they approached. He estimated their positions based on the sound . Pushing up, he leapt atop the bus. The old, rusty metal bent and flaked beneath him, but held his weight as he snapped to his first target. The reaver was only twenty-three and one-eighth feet away; well within range for the armor-piercing rounds to do their work. Ronin’s first burst caught the reaver square in the chest and punched through the casing beneath his synthetic skin. He fell before taking a return shot, limbs spasming . The other reaver reacted in the half-second Ronin’s attack allowed it, firing his rifle. The first bullets hit Ronin as he pivoted. He shook with the impact, but kept his arms steady and squeezed the trigger . The reaver’s gun bucked wildly, climbing with its uncontrolled recoil as Ronin’s shots pierced the bot’s casing and hit its power cell. Licks of blue fire erupted from the holes for a fraction of an instant. With smoke curling out of it, the reaver’s arms and torso went limp, its legs locking it in an upright position . Ronin held his weapon at the ready and approached the other bot. It twitched on the ground, kicking up little clouds of dust. Motor functions compromised. It had likely sustained damage to its CPU. Standing over it, he fired two more shots into its back. Its power cell flared in overload, and its thrashing ceased . He swept his optics over the area, detecting no further motion apart from that caused by the wind. If the reavers had any other companions, they’d decided the fight was too risky. He looked down to assess the damage he’d taken. His casing had deflected three of the bullets, but three more had penetrated. A diagnostic check indicated no significant damage to any of his systems . Ronin slung his rifle over his shoulder and checked the fallen bots for anything of value. Their rifles were pieced together from ancient parts — just like everything was, these days — but kept in such poor condition that he was surprised they had fired at all. He doubted they’d hold together over the short trip back to town . The ammunition looked reliable enough. He put the fourteen rounds into a coat pocket. His rifle wasn’t chambered for their caliber, but they’d have value in Cheyenne. Other than that, the bots had nothing worth taking. The undamaged parts in their casings would sell for good credit in Cheyenne or any other town, but Ronin left them be. It was illogical to think so, but something about pilfering a deactivated bot for pieces seemed wrong . He followed the skeletal, mostly-buried remains of automobiles back to the road. Cracked asphalt was still visible through the dirt, which was ever-moving thanks to the constant wind. More scrubby grass grew through the gaps. Soon, he left the old four-lane freeway and its dead vehicles behind, turning onto a side road leading directly to Cheyenne. Locals called it Camp Road, though its name had once been longer and less relevant . Here on the edge of the Dust, any path to shelter was one worth walking . In the eight months he’d spent based out of Cheyenne, Ronin had never encountered reavers so close to the town . He halted when he turned to look at the massive building to the north. Its walls had been battered by at least two hundred years of harsh weather, wearing away the paint and scratching the metal beneath. Twisted steel beams jutted from the partially-collapsed roof like the ribs of a decomposing pronghorn. All around the structure lay dozens of trailers — two hundred and fourteen of them, exactly. Thirty-seven were still hitched to the ancient machines that had once dragged them from one corner of the land to another . Decades of windstorms had deposited mounds of sediment around the building and the trailers alike. There was little left to salvage from the place. Ronin had searched each trailer at least twice, and gone through the accessible portions of the building four times. It was exactly fifteen hundred feet from end to end . Despite his thoroughness, he occasionally discovered small objects that held some value . He turned his optics to the west, waiting a moment for them to adjust to the glare of the evening sun. Five miles on, Cheyenne’s Bot District stood largely untouched by the ravages of time, quiet beneath the heat-shimmering air. Even at an easy pace, he could be back within an hour, surrounded by a sound roof and four sturdy walls instead of hundreds of miles of nothingness . Cheyenne wouldn’t go anywhere. It had stood since before the Black Out, before his circuits were scrambled and the world was undone. With or without Ronin, the bots and humans calling it home would continue their lives. And the sky, though perpetually gray and hazy, bore no storm sign . There was time enough for another brief search . He walked toward the ruined building, dirt crunching beneath his boots. Stopping at one of the battered doors, he grasped the latch with a bare-metal hand — the synthetic skin had long since been claimed by the Dust — and pulled. The door swung open with little resistance, groaning on its hinges . The building was dark and quiet inside. Most of the remaining materials were too large or impractical to remove — bundles of long, precisely cut wood boards now gray with age, sheets of crumbling dry wall, miles and miles of pipe and wire, and heavy sacks of dust that hardened when wet. Cement, his memory said, though the writing on the bags had long since faded beyond reading . Sometimes, there were heavy tread marks in the dirt outside. Ronin guessed that Warlord, the leader of Cheyenne, had a heavy-lifter or hauler of some sort to drag the large materials back to town when they were needed for repairs. Easy enough, with the right tools and relative nearness. But most of the bots in Cheyenne had been sheltered, bearing little understanding of how to scavenge, how to pull treasures from the Dust . Ronin adjusted the lay of his pack and swung his rifle into his hands. His thumb brushed over the smooth spot on the grip, worn over the fifty-seven years, four months, and twelve days it had been in his possession. He stepped forward slowly to keep his scrap from clanging, optics adjusting to compensate for the limited light . A distant, damaged memory told him this was a reception area, but he could not recall what its true purpose had been . Shafts of light pierced the ceiling in spots, illuminating floating motes of dust. The stuff was everywhere, in everything. That was reason enough to return to Cheyenne; he needed repairs before his insides filled with grit. That would, in time, finish the job the bullets had begun . After a quick search — most everything was in the same condition it had been the last time he was here, down to the undisturbed dirt on the floor — he moved around the broad, broken desk directly ahead of the entrance and through the door marked Associates Only . As he walked down the long hallway, the building’s walls groaned in the wind. Everything else was quiet. Ronin kept his advance slow, sweeping his optics from side to side, boots silent on the concrete floor. The small rooms along the hall had been mostly pilfered, the desks, chairs, and shelves inside battered and broken like most everything else in this world. One of the doors, removed from its hinges, lay in the hallway. That was different . He paused before crossing the open doorway, easing himself against the wall. The coating of dust was undisturbed, save for where it had been kicked up by the door falling, but that didn’t mean the place was empty. There were many, bots and humans alike, who would favor a place like this over the lights of Cheyenne . Numbers and walls did not always mean security . Leaning forward slightly, he peered past the jamb. His processors rapidly compared the room to the last reference in his memory. Nothing had changed, save for the fallen door. Satisfied, Ronin continued down the hall . Reaching the end, he let himself into the large room that had once served as a place for humans to congregate and eat. The long tables and chairs were overturned, legs broken off, and sunlight streamed in through the shattered windows. Weapon raised, he scanned the vicinity . Though the dust here was constantly shifted by the wind, there were boot prints clearly visible throughout. They wove between the tables and into the dark kitchen, and along the wall to the right, stopping at the restroom. The gait was uneven, the tread obscured in places due to the left foot dragging. Either a human or a damaged bot, though Ronin suspected even a damaged bot would have more regularity in its stride . He side-stepped to the counter, adjusting to night-vision to scan the kitchen. Someone had been through recently and torn it apart, as though there’d really be any food left after so long; by human count, the Black Out had been at least fifteen years before Ronin was woken by the Prophet, and Ronin was now in his one hundred and eighty-fifth year since reactivation. People had trouble preserving their crops for a few months , these days . Ronin crossed the cafeteria, following the tracks to the restrooms. He stopped at the door with the faded image of a circle on the top point of a triangle. It was the female restroom, though he didn’t understand why that data had survived the Black Out when so much else was lost. Keeping the stock of his rifle firm against his shoulder, he slowly pushed the door open with his left hand. Its rusty hinges whined in protest . There was no light inside; he switched his optics to infrared and swept the room. The stalls were all open, all empty, and the only heat he detected was from his own reflection in the shattered mirror — brightest where it seeped through the bullet holes in his casing . He moved directly to the men’s room. It was similarly cold; whoever had been here was gone. Reverting to his normal optics, Ronin drew his flashlight from an inside coat pocket and clicked it on . The remains of a small fire lay beneath the open ceiling vent, the wood charred black and crumbling. Nearby were a few tiny scraps of cloth, the dried-out bones of a small animal — a prairie dog, most likely — and a crumpled tin can. The dust wasn’t as thick in this room, but the disturbed spot on the ground indicated someone had lain near the fire . Ronin stepped carefully around the leavings and moved to the sink. The porcelain was yellowed and run through with fine cracks, but remained, surprisingly, in one piece. Within lay a few hand-rolled cigarettes, flakes of whatever plant had been used to make them gathered in the basin. A canteen stood on the flat part of the sink, nearest the mirror, wrapped in tattered canvas . Dark, rust-colored drops had dried around the edges and inside of the sink and soaked into some of the paper. Blood . An injured human had sheltered here, and either left or was taken in a hurry . Letting the shoulder strap take the weight of the rifle, Ronin picked up the canteen and shook it. Liquid sloshed inside. It didn’t have many uses for a bot, but it would for a human. He’d done good trade with them in the past. If not in Cheyenne, he’d trade it elsewhere. There were other towns on the edge of the Dust, and nothing truly tied him to Cheyenne . He slipped the container into a coat pocket. It clinked softly against the ammunition. Returning to the cafeteria, he turned the flashlight off and put it away, turning his optics to the sunlight. It had taken on the red-orange hue that signaled coming sunset . There were thousands more square feet to comb over, even without the trailers outside, but it would be best to head back. Minor as it was, his damage would only cause more complications the longer he ignored it, and his pack was full of scrap already . He’d spent 51,642 nights in the Dust and surrounding wilds since he was awoken. More than enough time to know that, despite night-vision and infrared optics, the world was even more dangerous after dark . Return, trade, recharge, repair. Then he could come back out again . * * * T he Bot District’s lights shone bright as Ronin closed in on Cheyenne’s eastern entryway. The buildings inside the wall stood in stark contrast to the ones without; most of the structures in the Bot District had been constructed before the Black Out, maintained over the years by automated units that had such tasks hard-coded in their programming. Many such bots existed in many towns. The majority lacked the higher cognitive functions of more advanced bots and synths . Still, Ronin envied them — they possessed a definitive purpose that hadn’t been claimed by the ravages of time, dictated directly by the Creators . Though the town’s upkeep wasn’t unusual — many towns with high concentrations of functioning bots had pre-Black Out buildings, in some cases with electricity and running water —the wall set Cheyenne apart. It surrounded the entirety of the Bot District, with a separate section containing the Market. Ten feet tall, it was an amalgamation of mismatched materials, many salvaged from the buildings that once stood outside its borders — sheet metal, wooden boards and planks, bricks, concrete blocks, and corrugated tin roofing . Ronin had only seen such structures erected by humans. This one protected only bots . The main road into Cheyenne followed mostly-buried train tracks. To the north stood the wall, sheltering the pristine buildings within and blocking everything from view save the light cast onto the clouds overhead. To the south were several old factories, now silent and still. A pair of guards waited in front of the roadblock between the two sections of town . One was a synth, the skin peeled off the top of his head to display the polished metal casing beneath. He called himself Reg. The other, Baron, was an earlier model bot, lacking an artificial epidermis. It was humanoid in shape, but its body was disproportioned; it could never be mistaken for an organic. Their rifles were amalgamations of mismatched parts that somehow came together as wholes. The weapons reminded Ronin of the wall in that respect . Ronin’s vision flared and darkened as his optics adjusted to the changing light . “Coming in late, dustwalker,” said Reg. The skull-like gear that Warlord used as his mark was clear on his head- casing . “Warlord doesn’t appreciate visitors after dark,” Baron explained. Its words were tinny, as though echoing within its vocal synthesizers. It wore Warlord’s mark on its chest, the paint faded and flaking . “But you already know that,” Reg concluded. “We’re simply obligated to inform you of the local customs and policies .” “Praise the Creators,” Ronin replied, “for they programmed you with far more patience than I. Warlord actually make you play these games every time, or have you two learned to take delight in it ?” The guards exchanged a glance . Reg lifted his chin. “What’s in your bag ?” “ Scrap .” “I expect vague answers from meatbags, dustwalker. What’s in it ?” Responses flitted through Ronin’s processors, assessed and discarded rapidly. It would be easy to deactivate these two. He had twelve rounds in his rifle; from this range, it wouldn’t take more than three for each bot to knock out their power cells. Even if they managed to get their rifles up quickly enough to return fire, their weapons were just as likely to explode as they were to shoot . And what then? It would accomplish nothing, would fulfill no directives, would bring Ronin no closer to the truth of his core programming . And Warlord had many more bots bearing that symbol in his service . Ronin could turn and walk away. The only thing tying him to Cheyenne were the few belongings left in his dwelling; they could be replaced, but everything in this world was too precious to abandon so readily . “Steel. Copper. Plastic. Damaged power cells .” “Quantities?” demanded Baron . “Will be determined by the scrapper,” Ronin replied . For five-and-a-half seconds, none of them spoke. The wind wailed over the wastes and the distant shouts of humans drifted over from the slums . “We need to send on word and wait for your entry to be approved,” Reg finally said, head twitching to the side once . “Fine. Be sure to send the answer down to Wellington, and explain to Warlord why my haul is being traded there instead.” Ronin turned on his heel. It was only thirty-seven miles away. He could be there by midnight if he maintained an easy pace. But Wellington was a much smaller community, and couldn’t offer the same price for goods as they did here . “Wait, dustwalker .” Ronin halted, slipping a finger behind the trigger guard. Most settlement-dwellers, whether mechanical or organic, were relatively fair in their dealings with him. The reavers kept to the Dust, for the most part. But Warlord’s town wasn’t normal in many ways . “ Enter .” Ronin didn’t immediately remove his finger from the trigger. He’d been pushing for a fight, he realized. Baiting, as humans sometimes called it. He forcibly eased his grip on the rifle and redirected himself toward Cheyenne . “You’d be better served behind the barriers. Picked up sight of you just over two miles out. Easy targets,” he said as he passed between the two guards and through the concrete barricade . They made no reply, but he saw them exchange a glance in his periphery . He walked in the shadow of the wall. The factory to his right had once been a refinery, but Warlord’s bots must have repurposed it; there hadn’t been any oil pumped in this region for at least two hundred years. The chain link fence around it was mostly intact, though it was a patchwork of rusted steel and new sections likely taken from the supply warehouse Ronin had stopped at on his way back to town . Steadily, voices came into audio range. The shouts of humans — most of the ones he could make out were calls for children to return home, as dark was quickly falling — and the drone of conversation from the Market. He didn’t divert any processing power to isolating and amplifying the individual voices. Their words didn’t matter. Everyone was simply doing their best to survive . Ronin just needed to offload his scrap and get repaired. Then he’d pick a new direction and start walking, come morning. He didn’t have to return to Cheyenne if he didn’t want to . As he walked, one sound caught his attention over the rest. He’d noticed it before, in this part of town, but had never investigated. A high-pitched, clanging tinkle; little bits of metal tapping together, perhaps . The first of the human shacks entered his view as the path shifted gently northwest, leading away from the fenced-in factory. Ronin glanced up briefly at the cracked remains of the roadway that once bridged the tracks. The north end would have led directly into the Bot District, long ago. Most of the rubble had been scavenged; only the most irregular chunks of concrete and rebar remained piled beneath the crumbled ramps . The sound grew more distinct as he neared the human dwellings. He couldn’t help but compare the shacks to the wall on the other side of the road. The resemblance was undeniable, when he broke them into their components. Both were hobbled-together declarations of defiance in a homicidal world, imperfect but somehow practical . He didn’t understand the logic chains his processors followed. No, he couldn’t consider them logic chains; there was little logical thought involved. Observations and baseless speculations. Nothing that did him any good . Yet wasn’t it that manner of thinking that allowed him to find what other dustwalkers had missed out in the wasteland ? A louder, higher clang claimed Ronin’s attention. He turned his head to the left. One of the shacks stood at the edge of the dirt-and-gravel pathway, and from its eaves dangled a metal loop with an eclectic array of items attached to it . He changed his course, walking to the shack. It was a simple device; the loop hung on a single piece of fishing line, and more lines of varying lengths suspended the forks, spoons, knives, and keys from the bottom of the ring. The objects tapped into each other in the wind, producing sounds of varying tones and pitches . Why would a human create such a thing? Did they find the noises it made appealing ? Another sound drifted to his receptors, this one from a living throat, wordless but distinct. Humming. A female human hummed with the chiming, matching its pace but not its notes, complimenting it without mimicking . Tilting his head to the side, Ronin approached the entry. A gap had been left between the door and the jamb, granting him view of the figure moving inside .
